questions.question: Define Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) and explain its fundamental working principle.
keyPoints: ABAC is an authorization mechanism that uses attributes instead of roles
Access decisions are based on subject, resource, action, and environment attributes
Policies define rules combining these attributes for dynamic access control
Provides fine-grained, context-aware access decisions

questions.question: Explain the four categories of attributes used in ABAC with one example for each.
keyPoints: Subject attributes: describe the user (e.g., role, department, clearance level)
Resource attributes: describe the object (e.g., file type, sensitivity level, owner)
Action attributes: describe the operation (e.g., read, write, delete)
Environment attributes: describe context (e.g., time, location, device type)

questions.question: Describe the functions of Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) and Policy Decision Point (PDP) in ABAC architecture.
keyPoints: PEP intercepts access requests from users
PEP collects necessary information and forwards to PDP
PDP evaluates requests against policies using attributes
PDP returns access decision (permit/deny) to PEP
PEP enforces the decision on the resource

questions.question: What is XACML? How is it related to ABAC?
keyPoints: XACML stands for eXtensible Access Control Markup Language
It is an XML-based standard language for expressing access control policies
XACML provides a standardized way to define ABAC policies
It enables interoperability between different ABAC implementations
Contains policy language (PAP), request/response protocol, and architecture definitions

questions.question: Compare ABAC and RBAC, highlighting why ABAC is more suitable for modern cloud environments.
keyPoints: RBAC uses static roles; ABAC uses dynamic attributes
ABAC supports fine-grained access control; RBAC is coarser
ABAC can consider context (time, location); RBAC cannot
For cloud: ABAC handles multi-tenancy, federated identities, diverse resource types better
ABAC supports complex policies needed for IoT and distributed systems

questions.question: List three advantages and three disadvantages of implementing ABAC in an organization.
keyPoints: Advantages: Fine-grained control, dynamic context-aware decisions, scalable for complex environments, supports federated access
Disadvantages: Complex to implement and manage, performance overhead, difficult to audit/debug, requires robust attribute infrastructure
Additional: Policy explosion problem, steep learning curve for administrators

questions.question: Explain the ABAC access request evaluation flow with the help of a diagram.
keyPoints: User initiates access request to resource
PEP intercepts the request
PIP retrieves required attributes from attribute sources
PDP evaluates request against policies
PDP returns permit/deny decision
PEP enforces the decision
Access granted or denied to user

questions.question: Explain the complete ABAC architecture including all components (PEP, PDP, PAP, PIP). How do these components interact to make and enforce an access control decision? Provide a detailed diagram description.
keyPoints: PEP: Intercepts requests, enforces decisions
PDP: Evaluates policies, makes decisions
PAP: Manages policies (create, store, administer)
PIP: Retrieves attributes from sources
Interaction flow: User→PEP→PIP→PDP→PEP→User
Attribute sources include LDAP, databases, APIs
PAP stores policies evaluated by PDP
PIP provides subject, resource, action, environment attributes
Each component can be distributed across the network
XACML standard defines interaction protocols

questions.question: Design an ABAC policy for a healthcare system that allows: (a) doctors to access medical records only of their assigned patients, (b) access only during hospital operating hours, (c) access only from authorized devices. Explain the attributes and policy rules.
keyPoints: Subject attributes: doctor_id, department, assigned_patients list, device_authorization
Resource attributes: medical_record_id, patient_id, record_sensitivity, record_department
Action attributes: access (read/write), export, print
Environment attributes: current_time, day_of_week, device_type, device_location
Policy rule 1: doctor.assigned_patients CONTAINS record.patient_id
Policy rule 2: environment.current_time WITHIN hospital_operating_hours
Policy rule 3: device.is_authorized = TRUE
Combine with AND logic: all conditions must be TRUE for access
Can be expressed in XACML format

questions.question: Discuss the challenges in migrating from RBAC to ABAC. How can an organization transition from RBAC to ABAC effectively?
keyPoints: Challenges: Complexity in policy design, performance overhead, attribute infrastructure requirement, staff training, policy migration effort, potential security gaps during transition

Transition approach: Inventory existing roles and permissions, identify attributes needed, select ABAC components, start with hybrid RBAC-ABAC, gradually move policies, implement attribute management infrastructure, test thoroughly, train administrators
Best practices: Start small, maintain role-to-attribute mapping, use XACML for policy standardization, implement policy versioning, regular audits

questions.question: Explain the n-ary ABAC model and discuss its significance in handling complex access control scenarios. How does it differ from basic ABAC?
keyPoints: Basic ABAC typically handles binary (permit/deny) decisions
n-ary ABAC supports multiple subjects, resources, actions, and environment conditions in single policies
Enables complex relationships: multiple users accessing multiple resources with different actions
Useful for collaborative workflows, group-based access, shared resources
Policies can express: 'User A and User B can access Document X for editing'
More expressive than simple subject-resource-action tuples
Enables delegation, group ownership, multi-party authorization scenarios
